---
title: "How Inference & Serving Work, Visually"
subtitle: "One tiny model, one sentence: real tokens, real logits, a real cache, real sampling, then two real calls to a real local server"
author: "Pranjal Rawat"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: black
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: true
    progress: true
    width: 1200
    height: 720
    margin: 0.055
    center: true
    navigation-mode: linear
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root {
  --bg: #16171a; --fg: #e6e6e9; --muted: #8b8b92; --sub: #a3a3ab;
  --accent: #7cb0de; --feat: #6bbf8a; --amber: #d9a441; --rule: #2a2c31;
  --node: #1d2733; --edge: #5a6472; --ink: #0f1114; --pink: #e06b9c;
  --violet: #b19cf5;
}
.reveal { font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif; }
.reveal, .reveal .slides section { background: var(--bg); color: var(--fg); }
.reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3 { color: var(--fg); font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: -0.5px; text-transform: none; }
.reveal h2 { font-size: 1.55em; border-bottom: 2px solid var(--rule); padding-bottom: 0.25em; margin-bottom: 0.5em; }
.reveal a { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal .slide-number { background: none; color: var(--muted); }
.reveal .footer, .reveal .slide-number { font-size: 12px; }
.reveal strong { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal em { color: var(--sub); font-style: italic; }
.reveal .subtle { color: var(--sub); font-size: 0.8em; }
.reveal .src { color: var(--muted); font-size: 0.55em; margin-top: 0.8em; }
.reveal .lead { font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.5; }
.reveal code { color: var(--feat); background: rgba(124,176,222,0.08); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 4px; }
.reveal section img { background: none; border: none; box-shadow: none; }
/* SVG shared visual language */
.fig { width: 100%; height: auto; max-height: 62vh; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.node { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; }
.node.feat { stroke: var(--feat); }
.node.hot { fill: #3a2f14; stroke: var(--amber); }
.node.ghost { fill: #191c20; stroke: var(--edge); }
.node.bad { stroke: var(--pink); }
.cap { fill: var(--fg); font: 16px -apple-system, sans-serif; text-anchor: middle; }
.cap.big { font-size: 21px; font-weight: 600; }
.zonelabel { fill: var(--muted); font: 600 14px -apple-system, sans-serif; letter-spacing: 1.5px; text-anchor: middle; }
.edge { stroke: var(--edge); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrow); }
.flow { stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrowA); stroke-dasharray: 6 5; }
.zone { fill: none; stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 12; }
.card { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 8; }
.card.hot { stroke: var(--amber); }
.card.ok { stroke: var(--feat); }
.card.bad { stroke: var(--pink); }
.cardtext { fill: var(--fg); font: 15px ui-monospace, monospace; }
.note { fill: var(--sub); font: 15px -apple-system, sans-serif; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.9); transition: all 0.3s ease; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in.visible { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); }
/* draw-on edges */
.edge.draw, .flow.draw { stroke-dasharray: 1; stroke-dashoffset: 1; }
.reveal .fragment.visible .edge.draw,
.reveal .fragment.visible .flow.draw,
.reveal .edge.draw.fragment.visible,
.reveal .flow.draw.fragment.visible { animation: gv-draw 0.55s ease both; }
@keyframes gv-draw { from { stroke-dashoffset: 1; } to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
.reveal .fragment.pop { opacity: 0; transform-box: fill-box; transform-origin: center; }
.reveal .fragment.pop.visible { animation: gv-pop 0.45s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.8,0.2,1) both; }
@keyframes gv-pop { 0% { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.5); } 60% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1.06); } 100% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); } }
.reveal .def-box, .reveal .key-idea, .reveal .pitfall { background: var(--node); border-radius: 8px; padding: .5em .8em; margin: .4em 0; border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); font-size: .82em; line-height: 1.4; }
.reveal .key-idea { border-left-color: var(--feat); }
.reveal .pitfall  { border-left-color: var(--pink); }
.reveal .def-box::before  { content: "DEFINITION"; }
.reveal .key-idea::before { content: "KEY IDEA"; }
.reveal .pitfall::before  { content: "PITFALL"; }
.reveal .def-box::before,.reveal .key-idea::before,.reveal .pitfall::before { display:block; font-size:.62em; letter-spacing:1.5px; font-weight:700; color:var(--muted); margin-bottom:.25em; }
.reveal .taxonomy { display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(var(--cols,3),1fr); gap:.6em; margin-top:.5em; }
.reveal .taxonomy .cat { background:var(--node); border:1px solid var(--rule); border-top:3px solid var(--accent); border-radius:6px; padding:.5em .7em; font-size:.74em; }
.reveal .taxonomy .cat h4 { margin:0 0 .2em; color:var(--accent); font-size:1.05em; }
</style>

<svg width="0" height="0" style="position:absolute" aria-hidden="true">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5a6472"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowA" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#7cb0de"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

# One call, taken apart {.center}

::: lead
"Inference" is one function call: tokens in, one more token out, repeated.
Everything a serving stack adds on top, caching, batching, sampling,
quantization, a server in front of it, is there to make that one call
cheaper or faster, never to change what it computes.
:::

Most figures ahead are photographs of gpt2 (124M params, CPU) really
completing one sentence, plus two real calls to a real local server, a
handful (7 of 39) are clearly-labeled concept cards, drawn to carry ideas
this tiny deck has no way to benchmark.

::: notes
The deck is one long argument in two parts. Part 1 opens up the single
forward pass: tokens, logits, the cache, batching, sampling, quantization,
and where a naive server stops scaling. Part 2 runs the same idea for
real against a serving stack: Ollama first, then litellm's one call shape,
then where vLLM takes over when Ollama is outgrown.
:::

---

## The running example

One sentence, one small model, completed token by token, the mechanics shown by real runs and a few labeled concept cards.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="140" width="260" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="82" y="172">"The cat sat on the"</text>
    <text class="note" x="82" y="202" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">a fixed sentence, tokenized</text>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="190" y="120">THE INPUT</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="node" x="390" y="115" width="220" height="140" rx="12"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="165">gpt2</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="195" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">124M params, CPU</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="213" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">torch.manual_seed(0), eval(), no_grad()</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card ok" x="680" y="130" width="260" height="110"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="702" y="162">logits -&gt; probs -&gt; a token</text>
    <text class="note" x="702" y="190" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">greedy, temperature, top-p</text>
    <text class="note" x="702" y="210" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">every number asserted by an oracle</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 320 185 L 385 185"/>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 610 185 L 675 185"/>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="320">Part 2 swaps gpt2-in-process for a real local server: Ollama, then litellm.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The honesty contract, same as the sibling decks: nothing here is scripted
or mocked. gpt2 really tokenizes, really runs forward, really caches,
really batches. Ollama and litellm really call a real qwen2.5:0.5b server
on localhost. Watch one recurring number: the KV cache's layer count, 12,
shows up again in the quantization topic's parameter count.
:::

---

# Part 1 · The mechanics of one token {.center}

::: lead
Everything in Part 1 happens inside a single process, on this laptop's
CPU: a string becomes ids, ids become logits, logits become a
distribution, and a growing cache and a batch dimension change nothing
about the answer, only its cost.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
Every topic ahead in Part 1 leaves the logits untouched: sampling only
changes how a token gets picked from them, and the cache and the batch
dimension only change what it costs to get there, never the answer.
:::

---

::: {.def-box}
A **token** is one piece of gpt2's byte-pair vocabulary, the atomic unit
the model actually reads and writes. The fixed sentence becomes a short
list of integer ids before anything else happens.
:::

---


# Tokenization

## Tokenization {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/tokenization/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Tokenization {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/tokenization/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Tokenization {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/tokenization/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## Tokenization {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/tokenization/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---

::: {.def-box}
A **logit** is the model's raw, unnormalized score for one entry in
gpt2's ~50,257-word vocabulary, one real number per candidate token,
before any softmax turns them into probabilities.
:::

---


# The Forward Pass

## The Forward Pass {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-forward-pass/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## The Forward Pass {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-forward-pass/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## The Forward Pass {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-forward-pass/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## The Forward Pass {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-forward-pass/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# The Logit Distribution

## The Logit Distribution {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-logit-distribution/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## The Logit Distribution {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-logit-distribution/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## The Logit Distribution {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-logit-distribution/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# Sampling

## Sampling {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/sampling/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Sampling {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/sampling/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## Three ways to decode the same logits

::::: {.taxonomy style="--cols:3"}
::: {.cat}
<h4>greedy</h4>
Always takes the argmax of the real logits. Deterministic: the same token every run.
:::
::: {.cat}
<h4>temperature</h4>
Divides the logits before softmax. This deck's temperature 1.6 spreads
probability mass and raises entropy versus temperature 0.7.
:::
::: {.cat}
<h4>top-p</h4>
Keeps the smallest sorted prefix of the distribution whose cumulative
mass reaches p, then renormalizes. A smaller p keeps a smaller, more
confident set.
:::
:::::

::: {.pitfall}
Greedy is the temperature-to-zero limit of the same softmax: deterministic,
always the same token. This deck's higher temperatures (0.7, 1.6) trade
that determinism for diversity, never for a different forward pass.
:::

---

::: {.def-box}
The **KV cache** stores each layer's past key and value tensors so a new
token's forward pass can attend to the whole prefix without recomputing
it from scratch.
:::

---


# The KV Cache

## The KV Cache {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-kv-cache/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## The KV Cache {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-kv-cache/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## The KV Cache {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-kv-cache/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## The KV Cache {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-kv-cache/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

## The KV Cache {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-kv-cache/step-05.png){fig-align="center"}

## The KV Cache {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-kv-cache/step-06.png){fig-align="center"}

## The KV Cache {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/the-kv-cache/step-07.png){fig-align="center"}

---

::: {.pitfall}
It is tempting to assume a growing cache changes the model's answer as
context grows. It does not: this topic's oracle proves the cached
next-token logits equal a full from-scratch recompute at every one of
the 4 generation steps, the cache only removes redundant computation.
:::

---


# Batching

## Batching {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/batching/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Batching {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/batching/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Batching {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/batching/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## Two ideas, before the code

The next two topics are concept-only: no serving engine lives in this deck to honestly benchmark, so no figure claims a speed number.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 260">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card hot" x="60" y="60" width="400" height="140"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="82" y="95">paged attention</text>
    <text class="note" x="82" y="125" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">the KV cache sliced into fixed blocks,</text>
    <text class="note" x="82" y="145" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">allocated like OS virtual-memory pages</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card hot" x="540" y="60" width="400" height="140"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="562" y="95">continuous batching</text>
    <text class="note" x="562" y="125" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">a finished request's GPU slot goes to</text>
    <text class="note" x="562" y="145" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">the next queued request immediately</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

---


# Why vLLM Is Fast

## Why vLLM Is Fast {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/why-vllm-is-fast/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Why vLLM Is Fast {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/why-vllm-is-fast/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Why vLLM Is Fast {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/why-vllm-is-fast/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

::: {.def-box}
**Quantization** stores each parameter in fewer bits, fp16 instead of
fp32, which changes how a number is stored and how much memory it costs
to move, never how many parameters exist or what they compute.
:::

---


# Quantization

## Quantization {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/quantization/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Quantization {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/quantization/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Quantization {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/quantization/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

::: {.def-box}
**Local vs hosted** is where the weights live. Hosted serving calls
someone else's GPUs and API over the network; local serving runs the
weights yourself, in this deck a real qwen2.5:0.5b behind Ollama on
localhost. Same call shape, different tradeoffs in control, privacy, and
who runs the ops.
:::

---


# Hosted vs Local

## Hosted vs Local {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/hosted-vs-local/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Hosted vs Local {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/hosted-vs-local/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

---

# Part 2 · Serving it for real {.center}

::: lead
Part 1 stayed inside one python process. Part 2 leaves it: a real HTTP
call to a real local model server, twice, then the one call shape that
also reaches a hosted provider, then where the throughput path takes over
once Ollama is outgrown.
:::

::: {.src}
Ollama and litellm calls in this section hit a real qwen2.5:0.5b server on localhost:11434, not a mock.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
Ollama, litellm, and vLLM are three ways to make the identical call
cheaper or faster to run at scale, never a way to change what it
computes.
:::

---


# Ollama, For Real

## Ollama, For Real {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/ollama-real/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Ollama, For Real {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/ollama-real/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Ollama, For Real {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/ollama-real/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# litellm, One Shape

## litellm, One Shape {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/litellm-one-shape/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## litellm, One Shape {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/litellm-one-shape/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## litellm, One Shape {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/litellm-one-shape/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# vLLM, The Throughput Path

## vLLM, The Throughput Path {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/vllm-the-throughput-path/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## vLLM, The Throughput Path {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/vllm-the-throughput-path/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## Three serving options, one call shape

::::: {.taxonomy style="--cols:3"}
::: {.cat}
<h4>local runtime</h4>
Ollama: a real HTTP call to a real local qwen2.5:0.5b server on
localhost:11434, right for one process serving one or a few users.
:::
::: {.cat}
<h4>gateway</h4>
litellm: wraps that same Ollama server behind the OpenAI-shaped request
and response every hosted provider also speaks, one call shape,
whichever provider sits behind it.
:::
::: {.cat}
<h4>throughput engine</h4>
vLLM: the path teams reach for once request volume outgrows what naive
batching on one GPU can absorb, via paged attention and continuous
batching (drawn, not run, in this deck).
:::
:::::

---

# The Whole Path {.center}

---

## One sentence, five ways to run it

Every figure in this deck traced one idea: the math stays fixed, only the cost changes.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 420">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="zone" x="40" y="40" width="280" height="220"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="180" y="68">ONE TOKEN (gpt2)</text>
    <rect class="card" x="65" y="85" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="110" style="font-size:13px">tokens -&gt; logits -&gt; probs</text>
    <rect class="card" x="65" y="135" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="160" style="font-size:13px">greedy / temperature / top-p</text>
    <rect class="card" x="65" y="185" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="210" style="font-size:13px">cache + batch: same answer</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="zone" x="360" y="40" width="280" height="220"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="68">THE FOOTPRINT</text>
    <rect class="card" x="385" y="85" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="405" y="110" style="font-size:13px">124M real params</text>
    <rect class="card" x="385" y="135" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="405" y="160" style="font-size:13px">fp16 bytes == fp32 bytes / 2</text>
    <rect class="card hot" x="385" y="185" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="405" y="210" style="fill:var(--amber); font-size:13px">paged attn + continuous batch</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="zone" x="680" y="40" width="280" height="220"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="820" y="68">SERVING IT FOR REAL</text>
    <rect class="card ok" x="705" y="85" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="725" y="110" style="font-size:13px">ollama.generate(...)</text>
    <rect class="card ok" x="705" y="135" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="725" y="160" style="font-size:13px">litellm: one call shape</text>
    <rect class="card" x="705" y="185" width="230" height="40"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="725" y="210" style="font-size:13px">vLLM: the throughput path</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="330">Two real calls, byte-identical at temperature=0, prove the determinism claim.</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="365" text-anchor="middle">Every other number in the deck comes from an oracle that reran the computation independently.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

---

## References

**Guides**
[Hugging Face, transformers documentation](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/) · [Ollama documentation](https://github.com/ollama/ollama/blob/main/docs/api.md) · [litellm documentation](https://docs.litellm.ai/) · [vLLM, PagedAttention paper (Kwon et al., 2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.06180)

**Siblings**
[How Git Works, Visually](../../how-git-works/website/git.html) · [How RAG Works, Visually](../../how-rag-works/website/rag.html) · [How Evals & Observability Work, Visually](../../how-evals-and-observability-work/website/evals.html) · [How Agentic Harnesses Work, Visually](../../how-agentic-harnesses-work/website/harness.html) · [How LangChain & LangGraph Work, Visually](../../how-langchain-and-langgraph-work/website/langchain.html)

::: {.src}
Most figures: real runs of gpt2 (transformers) plus real Ollama/litellm calls to a local qwen2.5:0.5b server; a handful are clearly-labeled concept cards. Regenerable with build/regen_all.py · versions pinned in build/requirements.txt
:::
